message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 13 (32 todo)
Recent changes: Green generalized block pricing — replaced whole-list isBlockOfThree with grouping by type (countByType) and per-group pricing (basePremiumForGroup): a component group of exactly 3 costs 60 G, otherwise each item charged at its base premium; main items fall through to base*count.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted. This was a substantive generalization — review naming and structure of the new grouping/pricing helpers carefully.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Let me first verify all tests are green before refactoring.

content.text:
All 13 tests pass. Now I'll work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase introduced three helpers. Let me evaluate each name against what the code now does:

- `countByType` — Returns a `Record<string, number>` mapping each item type to its count. Name accurately reveals intent.
- `basePremiumForGroup(type, count)` — Prices one group of same-type items. The name says "for group" but the parameters are a type and a count, not a group object. The name is reasonable but slightly abstract. Acceptable.
- `sumOfBasePremiums` — Sums base premiums across all type groups. Accurate.
- `componentTypes` — The set `{rune, moonstone}` — these are the types eligible for the block-of-three discount. The name "componentTypes" describes a domain category but does not reveal *why* the set exists in this pricing context (block eligibility). Mildly indirect, but matches domain vocabulary from the spec ("component building blocks").

**Naming assessment**: Names are clear and reveal intent. The most questionable is `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE` paired with the magic literal `3` in the condition `count === 3`. The `3` in the condition is an unexplained literal that should match the constant's name. This is a Rule 2 (reveal intent) opportunity.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focus on the changed/new pricing helpers (the substantive generalization):

**`countByType`** (lines 46-50):
- Bindings: `items`, `counts`, `item` = 3
- Invocations: `reduce`, `??` access pattern... counting `reduce` = 1 invocation = 2
- Conditionals: `??` (nullish coalescing) = 1 conditional = 4
- Assignments: `counts[item.type] = ...` = 1 = 6
- Loops: `reduce` = 1 = 5
- Constants: `0`, `{}` ≈ 1
- Subtotal ≈ 3 + 2 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 1 = 21

**`basePremiumForGroup`** (lines 52-57):
- Bindings: `type`, `count` = 2
- Invocations: `componentTypes.has`, `basePremiums[type] * count` (has=2, *=2) = 4
- Conditionals: `if`, `&&` = 2 = 8
- Constants: `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE` ref, `3` = 2
- Subtotal ≈ 2 + 4 + 8 + 2 = 16

**`sumOfBasePremiums`** (lines 59-65):
- Bindings: `items`, `counts`, `sum`, `type`, `count` = 5
- Invocations: `countByType`, `Object.entries`, `reduce`, `basePremiumForGroup`, `+` = 5×2 = 10
- Loops: `reduce` = 5
- Constants: `0` = 1
- Subtotal ≈ 5 + 10 + 5 + 1 = 21

**Total pricing-helper mass ≈ 58**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass)**: All 13 green.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent)**: The magic literal `3` in `count === 3` is disconnected from `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE`. A `BLOCK_SIZE = 3` named constant would tie the block concept together and make the rule self-documenting. This is the clearest improvement.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication)**: No meaningful duplication. The grouping/pricing split is clean.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements)**: The three-helper decomposition is justified — each has a single responsibility. No over-engineering.

The best refactoring is a **Rule 2** improvement: extract the magic `3` into a named `BLOCK_SIZE` constant.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

input.old_string:
const BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE = 60;

const componentTypes = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"]);

content.text:
All 13 tests still pass.

## Step 6: New APP Mass

The change swapped the constant literal `3` for a named binding reference `BLOCK_SIZE` in the conditional. The component count in `basePremiumForGroup` is unchanged (one constant literal `3` becomes one named-constant reference, both Mass 1). Net pricing-helper mass remains ≈ 58. **Mass change: 58 → 58 (Δ 0)** — this was a pure clarity (Rule 2) improvement, which correctly trumps APP.

## Step 7: Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring applied** (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):
- Extracted the magic literal `3` in the block-eligibility check into a named constant `BLOCK_SIZE = 3`. The condition now reads `count === BLOCK_SIZE`, tying the "block of three" concept together with `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE` and making the pricing rule self-documenting.

**Naming review of the Green-phase generalization** (carefully examined, no rename warranted):
- `countByType` — accurately returns per-type counts.
- `basePremiumForGroup(type, count)` — prices one same-type group; name fits.
- `sumOfBasePremiums` — sums group prices across types; accurate.
- `componentTypes` — matches the spec's "component building blocks" vocabulary.

**Rules 3 and 4**: No duplication found; the three-helper decomposition (group → price group → sum) is well-factored with single responsibilities and no over-engineering. No further change warranted.

**Mass change**: 58 → 58 (Δ 0) — clarity improvement with no mass cost.

**Tests**: 13 passing, 32 todo. All green.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test.
